shortDescription: An open-source, cross-platform JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine. It uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model, making it lightweight and efficient for data-intensive real-time applications.

topic: Why is networking in Node.js more scalable than file system I/O?
shortDescription: Networking is truly asynchronous at the OS level (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS, IOCP on Windows) — Node handles thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread. File I/O uses the libuv thread pool (default 4 threads), so only 4 heavy file operations can run in parallel before others queue.

topic: If Node.js is single-threaded, how can it use a multi-core CPU?
shortDescription: The JavaScript event loop is single-threaded, but libuv uses its thread pool for I/O and crypto. To run JS logic on all cores, use the cluster module (multiple processes, each with its own event loop) or worker_threads (true threads sharing memory).

topic: Does setTimeout(fn, 10) use a libuv thread?
shortDescription: No. Timers are managed directly by the event loop. libuv stores timers in a min-heap; each loop iteration checks the heap and fires any expired timers on the main thread. No background thread is involved.

topic: When does Node run a libuv callback after an async task completes?
shortDescription: Node follows a run-to-completion model — callbacks only execute when the call stack is completely empty. Even if libuv finishes a task, its callback waits in the queue until the current synchronous code finishes.

topic: How does the event loop handle setTimeout and setInterval callbacks?
shortDescription: Timer callbacks are processed in the Timers phase of the event loop. libuv stores them in a min-heap by expiry time. Each loop tick checks the heap and moves expired timer callbacks to the execution queue for that phase.

topic: If setTimeout and readFile complete simultaneously, which callback runs first?
shortDescription: The event loop's phase order decides: the Timers phase runs before the Poll phase (where I/O like readFile is processed). So the setTimeout callback generally executes first.

Schedules a callback to run immediately after the current operation, before the event loop advances to any of its 6 phases and before the Promise microtask queue. Used to ensure API consistency and to let EventEmitters emit safely after listeners are attached.

In the main module their order is non-deterministic (depends on a 1ms timer threshold). Inside an I/O callback, setImmediate always runs first because the Check phase follows immediately after the Poll phase. Note: setTimeout(fn, 0) is actually setTimeout(fn, 1).

Node offloads crypto and fs to libuv's thread pool. Increasing UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE raises concurrency for those tasks, but too many threads can degrade performance due to CPU context-switching overhead, thread contention, and hardware I/O bottlenecks.

The browser event loop is optimized for UI rendering: Macrotask → Microtasks → Render. The Node.js event loop (powered by libuv) has six phases (Timers, I/O, Idle, Poll, Check, Close) and a unique process.nextTick queue that runs before standard Promises.

CommonJS (require / module.exports) is Node's legacy synchronous module system. ES Modules (import / export) is the standardized modern system — static, supports tree-shaking and async loading. Node supports both but they cannot be freely mixed.

module.exports is the actual object returned by require(). exports is just a shorthand reference to it. Reassigning exports directly (exports = ...) breaks the reference and results in an empty export — always reassign module.exports instead.

ECMAScript is the spec JavaScript follows. Node.green is a compatibility table tracking which Node.js versions support specific ECMAScript features, helping developers know what syntax they can use without Babel.

Node uses the V8 Inspector protocol. Start with --inspect (attach debugger) or --inspect-brk (pause at first line). Chrome DevTools or VS Code can then connect to set breakpoints, step through code, and inspect the heap.

Axios: most popular, works in browser and Node, automatic JSON parsing and interceptors. Got: Node-only, strong retry logic and HTTP/2 support. Undici: powers Node's native fetch, highest raw throughput, best for microservices when used directly.

EventEmitter stores listeners in a _events object keyed by event name. When emit() is called, Node synchronously invokes all listeners on the current call stack — it does not defer to the event loop. This means a slow listener blocks the entire emit.

The fs module uses direct C++ bindings with callbacks — minimal object allocation. fs/promises wraps each operation in a Promise object, increasing memory overhead and adding microtask queue latency. The trade-off is cleaner syntax and easier error handling with async/await.
A function that takes one or more functions as arguments or returns a function. Possible because JavaScript treats functions as first-class citizens.

The period when a program is actively executing — when code runs and interacts with memory and I/O, as opposed to being compiled or written. Also refers to the runtime environment (e.g. Node.js, JVM) that provides resources for execution.
longDescription: Runtime errors occur during this phase when the program encounters unexpected conditions. The runtime environment manages memory allocation, garbage collection, I/O, and OS interactions — things the developer does not need to handle manually in high-level languages like JavaScript.

shortDescription: Google's open-source JavaScript and WebAssembly engine (written in C++) that powers Chrome and Node.js. It compiles JS directly to native machine code using JIT compilation.
longDescription: V8 handles memory management, garbage collection, and performance optimisations like inline caching and hidden classes. It makes JavaScript fast enough for server-side use. Node.js embeds V8 and adds APIs for file system, networking, and OS access that browsers do not expose.

shortDescription: Manages async operations by continuously cycling through six phases: Timers → I/O callbacks → Idle/Prepare → Poll → Check (setImmediate) → Close callbacks.
Node.js runs JavaScript on a single thread. Long-running synchronous operations block all I/O. Async patterns and the event loop exist to keep this single thread free.
I/O operations (network, disk) are initiated and the thread is immediately freed to handle other work. A callback or Promise is used to receive the result when it is ready.
A pattern for handling I/O-bound operations without blocking the thread. In Node.js this is done via callbacks, Promises, or async/await on top of the event loop.
Node Package Manager — the default package registry and CLI for installing, publishing, and managing JavaScript packages and project dependencies.
Self-contained units of code that encapsulate functionality and expose a public API. Node supports CommonJS (require) and ES Modules (import).
CommonJS function that synchronously loads a module, executes it, and returns its module.exports. The result is cached after the first load.
The object a CommonJS module exposes when required. Assign the value you want to export directly to module.exports.
The manifest file for a Node.js project. Defines the project name, version, entry point, scripts, dependencies, and devDependencies.
Auto-generated file that records the exact version tree of every installed dependency. Ensures reproducible installs across machines and CI environments.
Built-in modules included with Node: fs (file system), http/https, path, os, crypto, stream, events, child_process, cluster, process, url, buffer, timers.
Node's built-in class for implementing the observer/publish-subscribe pattern. Use on() to register listeners and emit() to trigger them synchronously.
Functions passed as arguments to be called when an async operation completes. The Node.js convention is error-first: callback(error, result).
An object representing the eventual completion or failure of an async operation. Provides .then() for success, .catch() for errors, and .finally() for cleanup.
Syntactic sugar over Promises. async functions always return a Promise. await pauses execution inside the function until the Promise resolves, without blocking the event loop.
Objects for reading or writing data piece-by-piece instead of loading it all into memory. Types: Readable, Writable, Duplex (both), Transform (modifies data in transit).
A fixed-size chunk of memory outside the V8 heap, used to handle binary data (file reads, network packets, crypto). Represented as a sequence of bytes.
A minimal, unopinionated web framework for Node.js. Provides routing, middleware support, and thin wrappers around Node's http module.
Functions with signature (req, res, next) that process a request in sequence. Each middleware either ends the cycle (sends a response) or calls next() to pass control forward.
Mapping HTTP methods and URL paths to handler functions. In Express: app.get('/path', handler) or router.post('/path', handler).
Building interfaces that allow software systems to communicate, typically over HTTP using REST or GraphQL.
In Express, error-handling middleware has four arguments (err, req, res, next) and is registered last. For async routes, errors must be explicitly passed to next(err).
Key-value pairs set outside the application to control behaviour — used for secrets, database URLs, and feature flags. Access via process.env.VAR_NAME. Never commit .env files to version control.
A global object providing information about and control over the current Node.js process: process.env, process.argv, process.exit(), process.pid, process.cwd().
Allows a Node app to fork multiple worker processes (one per CPU core), each running its own event loop. The master process distributes incoming connections across workers.
The child_process module spawns subprocesses to run shell commands or other scripts. Methods: spawn (streaming), exec (buffered), fork (Node process with IPC channel).

Provides APIs for reading, writing, deleting, and watching files and directories. Each operation has a callback version (fs.readFile), a Promise version (fs/promises.readFile), and a synchronous version (fs.readFileSync).
Provides cryptographic functionality including hashes, HMACs, ciphers, key generation, and digital signatures. Offloaded to libuv thread pool so it does not block the event loop.
setTimeout and setInterval schedule callbacks. setImmediate runs after the current I/O phase. process.nextTick runs before all of them, at the end of the current operation.
Created with http.createServer((req, res) => { ... }). req is a Readable stream; res is a Writable stream. Most production apps use Express or Fastify instead of the raw http module.
Same as http.createServer but requires SSL/TLS certificates (key and cert). In production, TLS is typically terminated at the load balancer or reverse proxy rather than in the Node process.
Full-duplex communication channels over a persistent TCP connection. The WebSocket handshake starts as HTTP and upgrades. Node libraries: ws (low-level), Socket.io (higher-level with fallbacks).
A library for real-time bidirectional communication. Adds rooms, namespaces, automatic reconnection, and graceful fallback to long-polling when WebSockets are unavailable.

Stateless authentication using signed tokens. The server signs a payload with a secret or private key; the client sends the token in subsequent requests. The server verifies the signature without querying a session store.
Delegated authorization protocol. Allows users to grant a third-party app limited access to their account on another service without sharing their password.

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser security mechanism that restricts which origins can call your API. Controlled via HTTP headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin). In Express, use the cors middleware.
Express middleware that sets security-related HTTP headers (Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, etc.) to protect against common web vulnerabilities.
Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks trick a logged-in user's browser into making requests to your server. Prevented with CSRF tokens (csurf middleware) or the SameSite cookie attribute.
Restricts how many requests a client can make in a given time window. Prevents abuse and DoS. In Express, use express-rate-limit. Often also implemented at the infrastructure level.
Server-side sessions store user state (e.g. login) keyed by a session ID sent to the client as a cookie. In Express: express-session with a store like Redis for production.

Small key-value pairs stored in the browser and sent with every matching request. Set via Set-Cookie header. Use HttpOnly to prevent JS access, Secure to require HTTPS, and SameSite to limit cross-origin sending.
ODM (Object Document Mapper) for MongoDB in Node.js. Provides schema definitions, validation, middleware (hooks), and a query builder on top of the native MongoDB driver.
Promise-based ORM for relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, MSSQL). Provides model definitions, associations, migrations, and query building.
Common frameworks: Jest (all-in-one), Mocha + Chai (flexible), Vitest (fast ESM-first). Supertest is used for HTTP endpoint testing. Node 18+ has a built-in test runner.
Use --inspect or --inspect-brk flags, then connect Chrome DevTools or VS Code. console.log is a last resort. node --trace-warnings helps trace deprecation sources.

V8 manages the heap with a generational garbage collector (Scavenger for short-lived objects, Mark-Sweep/Compact for long-lived ones). Heap size is limited (~1.5 GB by default). Use --max-old-space-size to increase it.
Use a structured logging library (Winston, Pino, Bunyan) instead of console.log in production. Structured logs (JSON) are easier to query in log aggregation tools like Datadog or ELK.
Compress HTTP response bodies (gzip or Brotli) to reduce transfer size. In Express, use the compression middleware. Best done at the reverse proxy layer in production.
Serve static assets in Express with express.static('public'). In production, serve static files from a CDN or reverse proxy (nginx) instead of the Node process.

Containerise a Node.js app with a Dockerfile: use node:alpine as base, copy package.json first (layer caching), run npm ci, copy source, and set CMD ["node", "server.js"]. Use .dockerignore to exclude node_modules.
Architecture where the application is split into small, independently deployable services that communicate via HTTP or message queues. Each service owns its data and can be scaled individually.
A query language and runtime for APIs where clients specify exactly what data they need. Reduces over-fetching and under-fetching. Apollo Server is the most popular Node.js implementation.
Read-Eval-Print Loop — the interactive Node.js shell. Run node with no arguments to enter it. Useful for quick experimentation and debugging.
Use nvm (Node Version Manager) or fnm to install and switch between multiple Node.js versions. Useful for matching the production environment or testing across versions.
